bins.name: State jurisdiction over retail interconnection
bins.desc: NARUC argues that the FPA exclusively reserves jurisdiction over retail load interconnection to the states, regardless of load size. FERC has never asserted this jurisdiction and the FPA bars it from doing so. Any final rule must affirmatively disclaim authority over distribution interconnections, generation, and retail sales, and must not override state decisions on contracting or retail wheeling.
quotes: It is imperative that FERC, in any final rulemaking, make clear that it is affirmatively not asserting jurisdiction over these facilities, or end-use sales which fall squarely within the exclusive jurisdiction of state energy regulatory authorities.

the Commission should avoid any action that would circumvent or negate state decisions governing the provision of retail service, including, but not limited to, determinations about contracting practices related to the assessment of generating costs to retail load and the permissibility of direct retail access or retail wheeling.

FERC has never attempted to assert jurisdiction over end-user load interconnections. The reason for this fact is that FERC asserting jurisdiction over load interconnection is outside the boundaries imposed by the FPA.

Section 201(b) of the FPA grants the states exclusive jurisdiction "over facilities used for the generation of electric energy or over facilities used in local distribution or for the transmission of electric energy in intrastate commerce, or over facilities for the transmission of electric energy consumed wholly by the transmitter."

If FERC were to assert jurisdiction over a service to a limited class of retail customers, it would interfere with the balancing performed by state regulators in retail rate cases, a determination that is solely a state decision, and which has significant rate impacts on all classes of customers.
Jurisdiction over large load interconnection falls squarely within the residual intrastate oversight originally undertaken by states and continuously exercised thereafter.

State public utility commissions retain jurisdiction over retail load interconnections, regardless of size, however, it is also true that the United States and its citizens will benefit from interconnecting large loads in a timely and non-discriminatory manner.

bins.name: Fair cost allocation to retail customers
bins.desc: NARUC supports ensuring that interconnection costs, including transmission upgrades, are allocated fairly and do not impose undue burdens on existing retail ratepayers. Federal assertion of jurisdiction over a subset of retail customers would disrupt state rate-balancing across all customer classes.
quotes: If FERC were to assert jurisdiction over a service to a limited class of retail customers, it would interfere with the balancing performed by state regulators in retail rate cases, a determination that is solely a state decision, and which has significant rate impacts on all classes of customers.
the costs of large load interconnections, including necessary transmission upgrades and other infrastructure investments, may unfairly burden retail ratepayers if not properly allocated

bins.name: State flexibility tools should be preserved
bins.desc: At least 20 states have approved or pending large load tariffs with financial commitments, curtailment protocols, and minimum contract terms. NARUC supports preserving these state flexibility mechanisms rather than displacing them with federal rulemaking.
quotes: at least 20 states have approved or have pending large load tariffs or similar measures, which may include financial commitments, curtailment protocols, and minimum contract terms to allow for the rapid interconnection of large loads without compromising grid reliability or unduly burdening existing retail customers

bins.name: Preserve state retail regulatory authority
bins.desc: NARUC emphasizes that state commissions must retain authority over retail contracting practices, cost assessment to retail load, and determinations about retail access. Federal action that circumvents these state decisions would harm all customer classes, not only large loads.
quotes: the Commission should avoid any action that would circumvent or negate state decisions governing the provision of retail service, including, but not limited to, determinations about contracting practices related to the assessment of generating costs to retail load and the permissibility of direct retail access or retail wheeling.

If FERC were to assert jurisdiction over a service to a limited class of retail customers, it would interfere with the balancing performed by state regulators in retail rate cases, a determination that is solely a state decision, and which has significant rate impacts on all classes of customers.

bins.name: FPA statutory limits on FERC authority
bins.desc: NARUC cites the specific text of FPA section 201(b) to establish that states hold exclusive jurisdiction over local distribution, intrastate transmission, and generation facilities. This statutory reservation forms the legal foundation for NARUC's jurisdictional arguments.
quotes: Section 201(b) of the FPA grants the states exclusive jurisdiction "over facilities used for the generation of electric energy or over facilities used in local distribution or for the transmission of electric energy in intrastate commerce, or over facilities for the transmission of electric energy consumed wholly by the transmitter."

bins.name: Cooperative federal-state engagement
bins.desc: While opposing federal encroachment, NARUC pledges to work collaboratively with FERC and stakeholders toward consensus solutions. NARUC requests that FERC convene a joint federal-state forum to examine challenges and goals before issuing a final rule.
quotes: State public utility commissions retain jurisdiction over retail load interconnections, regardless of size, however, it is also true that the United States and its citizens will benefit from interconnecting large loads in a timely and non-discriminatory manner.

NARUC pledges to engage with regulated entities and other stakeholders to explore consensus solutions for FERC's consideration that will help meet national goals for large load interconnection, while avoiding disputes over jurisdiction that would impede achieving our shared goals.
NARUC respectfully requests that the FERC bring federal and state regulators together for a detailed examination of the challenges and goals.

bins.name: Reliability risk to existing customers
bins.desc: NARUC warns that connecting large loads without sufficient generation capacity could threaten reliable service to existing retail customers during peak demand or extreme weather events. This concern supports the case for careful state-level oversight of interconnection decisions.
quotes: any large load interconnections without sufficient available generation capacity could threaten reliable power service to existing retail customers, as grid operators may lack the resources needed to maintain system stability during peak demand or extreme weather events

bins.name: Interconnection cost burden on ratepayers
bins.desc: NARUC flags that transmission upgrade costs and other infrastructure investments tied to large load interconnections may unfairly shift onto retail ratepayers if not properly allocated, reinforcing the need for state commission oversight.
quotes: the costs of large load interconnections, including necessary transmission upgrades and other infrastructure investments, may unfairly burden retail ratepayers if not properly allocated

bins.name: Existing state large load tariff programs
bins.desc: NARUC notes that at least 20 states already have approved or pending large load tariffs covering financial commitments, curtailment protocols, and minimum contract terms. These programs demonstrate that states are actively managing large load interconnection without federal intervention.
quotes: at least 20 states have approved or have pending large load tariffs or similar measures, which may include financial commitments, curtailment protocols, and minimum contract terms to allow for the rapid interconnection of large loads without compromising grid reliability or unduly burdening existing retail customers
